attribution: Original text authored for this fixture, 2026

paragraphs.text: Sela the snail wanted to be fast. She watched the rabbits race past the garden gate. Zip, zip, zip! "Wait for me," called Sela. But the rabbits never waited. So Sela practiced every morning. She slid down the cool side of the watering can. She slid across the big flat stone. She was still slow. But one rainy day, the rabbits hid under the hedge, and Sela kept going, slow and steady and smiling, all the way to the carrot patch. She got there first.

Under the bed lived one blue sock. The sock had lost its brother on washing day. Every night it listened for footsteps. Every night it hoped. One morning a small hand reached under the bed. "Found you!" said Milo. He held the blue sock up to the light. In his other hand was the brother sock, fresh from the laundry basket, still warm. The two socks went onto two feet, and the two feet went jumping down the stairs, thump, thump, thump, all the way to breakfast.

Pip was a field mouse with a big idea. He had heard the old barn owl say that the moon was made of cheese, and Pip loved cheese more than anything in the whole wide field. So Pip packed a bag. He packed a crumb of bread, a thimble of water, and his warmest scarf. He climbed the tallest sunflower in the meadow, higher and higher, until the petals brushed the evening sky. The moon hung above him, round and yellow as a wheel of cheese. Pip stretched out his paw. He stretched and stretched, but the moon stayed far away. Then Pip looked down. Below him, the pond held a second moon, wobbling softly on the water. Pip laughed. "I cannot eat the moon," he said, "but I can share my bread with the fish, and that is nearly as good." And he did, crumb by crumb, while the moon watched over them both.

Juni found a puddle that held the whole sky. It sat in the middle of the lane after the rain, round as a dinner plate, and inside it floated the clouds, the birds, and one surprised airplane. "Excuse me," said Juni, kneeling down, "is the sky comfortable in there?" The puddle said nothing, because puddles are shy. Juni decided to keep the puddle safe. She stood guard while the postman stepped around it. She shooed away a thirsty dog. She warned two boys on bicycles, and the boys rang their bells and rode along the grass instead. All afternoon Juni guarded the sky in the puddle. But the sun came out, warm and bright, and the puddle grew smaller and smaller. Juni did not feel sad. The sky was going home, she decided, one sip at a time. When the puddle was gone, she looked up. There was the sky, safe and wide and blue. "You're welcome," said Juni, and she skipped home for supper.

The bakery cat was named Crumb, and Crumb took her job very seriously. Every morning before the sun woke up, she marched along the counter, past the buns, past the rolls, past the tall white loaves. She checked the flour sacks for mice. She checked the window for pigeons. She checked the baker's apron pocket, because once it had held a sardine, and a good guard never forgets a sardine. One morning, a small girl pressed her nose against the bakery window. She had no coins, only a paper boat she had folded herself. Crumb looked at the girl. The girl looked at Crumb. Then Crumb nudged a warm bun off the counter, very gently, as if by accident. The baker laughed and gave the bun to the girl, and the girl gave Crumb her paper boat. It sits in the window still, sailing nowhere, guarding the buns.

Three frogs lived in the pond at the bottom of Murk Meadow, and they argued about everything. Big Frog said the best lily pad was the wide one in the sun. Middle Frog said the best lily pad was the bouncy one by the reeds. Little Frog said nothing at all, because Little Frog was busy. All summer the two big frogs argued. They puffed out their throats. They croaked their loudest croaks. The dragonflies got headaches and moved to another pond. Meanwhile, Little Frog gathered duckweed, one green speck at a time. She wove the specks together with strands of soft pond grass. By the first cool day of autumn, Little Frog had made a lily pad of her very own, just her size, anchored in the quiet corner where the willow dipped its branches. Big Frog and Middle Frog stopped arguing and stared. "Can we visit?" they asked. "Yes," said Little Frog, "one at a time, and only if you bring snacks." And that is how the pond grew peaceful, mostly.

On the rock at the edge of the bay stood a small white lighthouse, and in the lighthouse lived a keeper and his daughter, Maren. Every evening Maren climbed the ninety-nine steps to light the great lamp. One stormy night, the wind blew so hard that the lamp went out, and the matches were wet, every last one. Far away on the dark water, a fishing boat was trying to find its way home. Maren did not cry. She opened her treasure tin. Inside lived Spark, a lantern beetle she had rescued from a spider's web in spring. "Spark," said Maren, "I need the biggest glow you have ever glowed." Spark crawled to the very top of the cold glass lamp. He thought about warm summer nights. He thought about Maren's kind hands. Then he glowed, brighter and brighter, until the whole lamp room shone like a small gold star. It was not a big light, but the sea was very dark, and even a small light goes a long way when it is the only one. The fishing boat turned, found the harbor, and tied up safe. In the morning, the fishermen left a basket by the lighthouse door. In it were three fine fish for the keeper, a ribbon for Maren, and one drop of honey on a clean white plate, for the bravest beetle in the bay.

Tovo the tortoise collected sounds. Other animals collected shiny stones or sweet berries, but Tovo walked the wide world with a basket of little glass jars, and into the jars he put the sounds he loved best. He had the plip of rain landing on a mushroom. He had the creak of the old oak stretching in the morning. He had the giggle of a brook that had just told itself a joke. Whenever Tovo caught a sound, he wrote a tiny label and tied it to the jar with string. The other animals thought this was a strange hobby. "Sounds are everywhere," scoffed the magpie, who collected spoons. "Why keep them in jars?" Tovo only smiled and plodded on. Then came the long winter, the longest anyone could remember. Snow lay thick on the meadow. The brook froze and stopped giggling. The oak stood stiff and silent. The rain turned to quiet snow, and the world went still as a held breath. The animals gathered in the big burrow under the hill, and the silence sat on them like a heavy blanket. Nobody could sleep. Nobody could remember what spring sounded like. So Tovo opened his basket. He opened the first jar, and out came the plip, plip, plip of rain on a mushroom. He opened the second jar, and the old oak creaked and stretched. He opened the third jar, and the brook giggled, and soon the whole burrow was giggling with it. Jar by jar, Tovo let spring out into the winter dark, and the animals closed their eyes and listened until they fell asleep, every one. In the morning the magpie was first awake. She tapped Tovo gently with a spoon. "About your hobby," she said. "I have decided it is the best hobby. Will you teach me?" And Tovo did.

The wind lost its whistle on a Tuesday. It had been whistling through the village all morning, rattling the gates, spinning the weathervane, lifting the laundry into a dance. Then, somewhere between the bakery and the bell tower, the whistle slipped away, and the wind could only huff. A wind that cannot whistle is a sad thing. It moped along the rooftops. It sighed down the chimneys. The laundry hung still, and the weathervane pointed nowhere in particular. A girl named Oda noticed first, because Oda noticed everything. She found the wind sulking in the alley behind her grandmother's house, stirring one crisp brown leaf in slow, gloomy circles. "You've lost your whistle," said Oda. The wind huffed, which meant yes. "I lose things too," said Oda. "I lost a tooth, a marble, and my left mitten. Grandmother says lost things are not gone, only waiting." So Oda and the wind went looking. They looked in the bell tower, where the wind found an old echo, but no whistle. They looked under the bridge, where the wind found a shiver, but no whistle. At last they came to the bakery, and there, sitting on the windowsill, was a kettle just beginning to boil. The kettle whistled. The wind leaned closer. The kettle whistled louder, high and bright and bold, and the wind drew one long breath and copied it, just like that. The whistle came out a little different than before, a little kettle-ish around the edges, but the wind liked it better. It whistled up the street and down again. The laundry danced. The weathervane spun. And every Tuesday after that, the wind stopped by the bakery window, just to whistle along with the kettle, while Oda ate a warm roll and listened to her two friends sing.

Little Cloud was learning to rain. Mother Cloud showed her how. "Gather up your gray," said Mother Cloud, "hold your breath, and let go." Little Cloud gathered up her gray. She held her breath. She let go. Out came one drop. It landed on a daisy, who said, "Thank you very much." Little Cloud practiced all week. Two drops on Monday. A pitter on Wednesday. A patter on Friday. By Sunday she could make a proper shower, soft and silver, just the kind gardens love. The daisies stood up straight. The carrots grew sweet. And down in the lane, a girl in red boots jumped in the puddles, shouting, "More, please!" Little Cloud puffed up proudly and rained her very best rain, while Mother Cloud rumbled, far away and gentle, like applause.
